sentences.text: Thank you, Leah.
I-- after, after Russians captured the city and everything settle down, the Allied troops came into, into Berlin, moved into Berlin on July 4th.
And that was quite a joyous relief for the population of Berlin.
But the Russians did-- while they had the-- while they were the sole occupiers of Berlin, they did a lot of good things, too.
I mean, they-- no bread was in the city.

And they could-- they-- we-- one of the first things they opened, reopened the German bakeries and, and made them work and supply the flour and so forth, and so that the, that the people would have something to eat.
When-- as the-- as everything settled down, the British were, and the French and Americans had moved into their sectors.
Kurt, my friend from the camp, and I decided-- both of our families-- well, he knew for sure that his family was in Bavaria.
But I didn't know for sure.
But I knew that my mother and brother had gone underground.
Because my brother was supposed to be put into one of those camps that I had been in.
So they were in underground and lived somewhere in Bavaria.
We decided--
No.
We-- the mail, mail was very bad.
I did get some mail.
And they made an indication.
But they would never really say where they would go or when they would g.
Because the mail would be censored, you know, and that would, that would be-- would have been unwise.
So I-- we decided to try out-- which was very difficult because there was no transportation.
You know, trains were all just-- no trains were running.
You had to hitchhike rides with-- we, I think we did catch a train up to the-- at the city near where we had our camp, near the Alba River.
And then-- but then it stopped.
Because all the railroad bridges were out, destroyed over the Alba River.
So we had to walk over one.
And it was kind of, you know, different.
You had to go past Russian guards.
We did have a paper that said that we were survivors.

I don't know if said-- it probably said just Jewish or survivors-- that was written in German and Russian for the benefit of any Russian guides, that we had gotten from one of the, you know, Russian authorities, Russian-German authorities now.
And so that that was our best identification.

And we went tracking through Germany by foot, railroad, crossing the Alba River bridge, and then going through the rest-- again, on hitchhiking rides, getting an occasional train ride, until we got toward the zone border between Russia, the Russian occupation and US occupation, Bavaria on the other side.
And here, we had to stop.
Nobody was, nobody was supposed to go either-- in either direction.
It was total-- there had been no agreements yet in how to handle the transit across the different zone borders.
But we didn't want to wait.
So the very first time we started out, we met up in one of the cities nearby the zone border.
And we met up with some-- two German soldiers, ex German soldiers, that also had wanted to go into Bavaria, and a young girl.

And the five of us, which was a mistake-- we were much too big a group-- we tried to get up, work our way up to the forest that was the-- before the opening where the border-- which was not really marked.
You just had to assume there.
You know, there were no-- really no guards.
I mean, every so often there was a guard house, every maybe every mile or every kilometer or something like this.
Oh yeah, the war-- that's, that's August of 1945, yeah.
And so we go, we go on.
And we-- we're waiting in a, in a, in a forest, at the edge of the forest, to cross that opening, that big field-- on, on the other side is the Americans zone-- while the morning fog is on.
And standing there about 4:00.
It was August, so daybreak starts early.
We're starting to walk across, actually running.
We have knapsacks on our, on our backs, when the fog lifts.
And, and the, and the Russian patrol spots, spotted us and started shooting, yelling to, to stop, to halt.
We don't do this.
We start running toward the forest, hoping we will get away and get lost in the forest.
But they were-- it was foolish, really.
Because they were shooting after us.
And the bullets were whizzing around all.
But they were bad shots.
So-- but they were better runners, so they caught up with us.
And they brought us back to the command post near the zone border, near the-- on the Russian side.
And there, we had to go through the routine again, Showed.
Our papers.
The, the officer in charge let us go, but with a stern warning, you have to go back to Berlin.
There's no agreement.
You cannot-- I said, well, my family and everybody, we're survivors.
He said, I don't care.
So then the next time we tried it, and we were successful.
We got over.
And we, we found out-- we went back to the city and did a little bit investigating and found that there are German farmers who have their fields on the other side of the zone border.
Because that was arbitrarily set up.
So they're making a business.
They're smuggling people over for money.
Well, we had money.
So-- How did you have money?
Well, we all-- we had some money.
Because there was-- from the-- you know, I had some money that was left over when I was in the camp.
I had money.
I had camp-- I had money with me.
My mother had given me money before I left to the camp.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I had it on my wallet.
And then, then I had-- then she had some money, I think, hidden away in the apartment where we were, just in case that I would come back there.
I mean, I-- then I got money from some of the other people that would give me money, that, that I helped survive.
And, and, you know, and getting-- being-- you know, in my, my dealings with the Russians that I, that I had when-- in, in Berlin when the very first days, you know?
No, money was no problem.
I mean, we had, we had money.
Because we paid this farmer.
And he took us over, both dressed as, as farm hands.
I had to take my glasses off.
Because he said even the Russian guard is not that stupid that he knows a farmhand that is as near-sighted and wears glasses like that.
They look yoo intellectual for him.
So I did.
It was pretty, pretty exciting, because we had, we had to walk right past.

And we picked the right moment, 6 o'clock, just before 6 o'clock in the morning, when the guy, the Russian guy who-- guard who has been on duty all night long is just in bad-- you know, anxiously awaiting his replacement.
And he really pay-- doesn't pay attention.
He knew the farmer had the right to.
And he has-- can always get different farmhands.
So we walked past him.
And it still was the, the longest 25 feet that I ever walked between that, that, you know, that Russian guard house.
And then there was a tent on the other side.
And there were American GIs in there.
And they didn't say anything, you know?
So then you [INAUDIBLE]?
Yeah, it was only Americans.
Yeah, but it was still different because-- difficult because our luggage, our knapsacks, were on the-- on a hay wagon, on the, on the, you know, on the bottom of a hay wagon.
And he would bring that over to us later on and meet us at the-- a great place on the American side.
And while we were waiting for him, the Russians were-- they knew that people would constantly cross.
It was a wide open border.
It was no [? marking. ?] They would infiltrate into the American territory and try to bring back people, catch people like us.
So we were hiding there for a while.
But everything went well.

We went to the first big city near the Bavarian-- on the US side, where they had-- went to an American [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] command post there, got out, showed him our identification from, from Berlin, and got ourselves new identification for the US zone that we could travel freely.
Cause-- and then we went on.
I, I searched for my family.
And we split then.
We went both to Munich, and he went on to this Bavarian resort where his parents lived.
And I started looking for my, for my family.
All I knew is I thought they were in Bavaria.

And there was one thing that we had agreed with my uncle, Arno Summerfeld, if we get lost, if we get separated in the war, leave a letter in general delivery at major post offices-- in major-- in the main post office in major cities.
Well, my instinct told me they went to Bavaria.
That's because that's where we had our sanatorium and where she could hide out.
And people that were the administrators are a very loyal couple.
They would hide them.
So I went to--
I was right.
I went to the-- and sure enough, there's a letter there, addressed to me, waiting in general delivery, telling me they're living in an apartment right in Munich.
Well, it was a kind of a bittersweet reunion.
Because I first had to see my uncle.
I didn't even go to my mother's-- my uncle and aunt.
And they told me my mother would be so upset.
She would be so overjoyed that they were afraid she would have a heart attack if she sees me.
Because they assumed I might have been dead.
They didn't know what the end of these camps were.
So they, they-- we had a wonderful, you know, reunion.
And then I, then I took it early.
It was like mid-August of '45.
Then I said to my uncle, I said, what, what do you want to do?
He said to me, what do you want to do?
He's a-- and he was a writer, an author, and a playwright.
And I said I really wanted to study medicine, wanted to finish my education.
Because everybody in my family-- I come from three generations of medicine.
He says, listen, you're a good talker, a good writer, a German.
He says, why don't you become a journalist?
And a paper just opened up, the first paper ever to open up in, in post-war Germany, run by the American military in German language, because there was no paper available for the Germans.

So I-- he says, why don't you go-- why don't you-- I, I already talked to them from himself, because he wanted to sell them some short, short stories for their, for their culture, you know, for their entertainment section.
So I know them.
He sent me to one of the guys there.
And the man says to me, you don't have you high school diploma yet.
I said because I was put in a camp.
I was thrown out of school.
He says, well, you're too young.
You go back to, you go back to college two years, get your high school diploma first.
And then go to college two years and then come back.
Well, I didn't take, really, no for an answer.
Because I, I really wanted to do, I wanted to do it.
And I really got interested in journalism.
So two weeks later, I went back to see somebody else at the paper, same paper.
The man hired me on the spot.
He became a lifelong friend.
He's still-- now he's retired.
He's In his 70s.
And he--
No he's-- yeah, he's a fellow German.
But he was smart.
He was back as one of the American officers.
He was a-- came to the US as a 16-year-old in 1938 and studied at Yale.
He had relatives here.
He's half Jewish, too.
But he's not Jewish.
He's Catholic, I think.
But that's-- no, he left, too, because of the persecution.
But he came in 1937.
He was back as an American officer in a US uniform, you know?
So he hired me, yeah.
And I started working.

And I worked there for travel all over Germany in a US military car with a German driver, and covering war crime trials, DP camps, anything of, of topics, you know, of events that happened in the post-- my, my beat was, my regular beat was the Bavarian government.
I interviewed the, the Bavarian prime minister, the economics-- that was my regular beat, going around every day and, and, and talking to the newly-formed Bavarian authorities.
That was very interesting.
There were two types, the Nuremberg war crime trials, which was, I think, began in '46.
I, I did not really go to that trial, but I followed.
Because one of the Americans, German-Americans on the paper, covered that.
That was, you know, a, a major thing.
But they sent me to, they sent me to what-- the denazification courts, where Germans judge Germans.
They were to make-- to judge their behavior during the Nazi times.
If they had done anything wrong, if they had, you know, acted unethically, and so forth.
And I, I was kind of disappointed with those courts.
Because they were not-- I, a lot of times I thought they were whitewash things.
And in fact, that, that's one of the things, as I, as I kept working there and seeing all this.
And then the next thing I see, the head of the, the head of the German Bavarian Red Cross, which is a very important organization, suddenly turns out, he's a former member of the Nazi party.
And then I see people there and there, in important positions.
I see businesses open up.
People that everybody knew, but nobody cares.
They go and coming back.
And it's business as usual.
Nothing has happened.
They were Nazis.
They were not involved in atrocities.
They were just fellow travelers.
But they were opportunists.
They took advantage of being.
Now they were back in business.
So I watched all of this.
And I got more and more embittered.
I, I could not believe what's going on.
And I said to my mother, I said, I can't take that anymore.
If I stay here, I'm going to get, get ulcers.
Because this is so much injustice.
It's so unfair that these people, even though they didn't do anything wrong, I mean they, they gained from all these things, you know, from being a Nazi party member.
They were privileged.
They had privileged status.
So they're all-- they were all-- you know, and suddenly they're all back in business.
So I decided, there's no way that I can stay here.
So I had my choice.
I go to-- I went.
And I had contacts.
I contacted the Jewish Joint Distribution Committee.
And I-- it turned out that I quit my job in the newspaper after traveling all over Germany and covering events for them.
I just did some freelance writing, because now I had to make the decision.
I was going to go and leave Germany.
And I was-- I had relatives that were smart enough, come over here.
And they were in Chicago, actually.
A physician who worked-- a cousin of my father's, who worked at-- one who worked at the VA hospital in Memphis, Tennessee, or Nashville, Tennessee.
And the other one was here, right here, in [INAUDIBLE].
They came over 1937-38.
So, but I-- they really weren't much help.
I asked them if they would sponsor me.
And they said, well, they talked about it, talked around about it.
They sent me a number of care packages and other things, which was nice.
Because you couldn't get anything in Germany until 1948.
It was very rationed.
So we went-- I decided I'm qualifying to be a displaced person.
They changed the displaced persons thing-- requirements.
You could include a German nationality, which I don't know why they didn't do it from the beginning.
Harry Truman had something to do with it.
He was present of the US then.
The Germans were-- German Jews were just as persecuted as Polish or Lithuanians and so forth.
So they changed it.

So then, then I worked for-- then I got a job, a job offer to work the last six months before my-- it takes about six months to get processed-- with the-- for the Joint Distribution Committee in Munich, at Munich headquarters.
And that was, was a very accommodating, because that was the agency that was going to take me through all the steps.
And they decided it.
So I came over in August 5th, 19-- I landed in New York Harbor on August 5th, 1951.
I'm sorry, yeah, 1951.
And somebody from the joint distribution committee picked me up at the, at the pier.
I actually volunteered.
I was one of the last ones.
Because I volunteered, because I spoke English well enough that-- to translate for many of the-- German was a good language.
But many of these people were speaking Polish, Lithuanian.
But many of them could understand and communicate in German.
So they used me for the medical officer for the Public Health Service officer, checking the medical records.
It was right there.
Yeah, it was right on the pier.
Yeah, but no-- yeah, no, no, I worked for the Joint Distribution Committee in, in Munich.
Munich, right.
And then my, then my immigration, you know, was-- came through, my, my visa and-- well, you don't-- you didn't need a visa.
You just needed your DP certificates and, and travel orders.
And then I came-- was sent to Williamsport, Pennsylvania.
That was assigned, pre-assigned to me.
By the Joint?
Yeah, by the Joint.
They had to go.
I didn't even know where Williamsport-- when I got the information, the notice that I was sent there, I had to go on a map.
And you know, I didn't, I didn't really know that much.
I knew where New York, New Orleans and San Francisco were, but not Williamsport.
I found it was very good.
It's was a small-- it's a small city in Pennsylvania.
It's actually the birthplace of the little league.
It's that city's claim to fame.
And I had a, I had a great time there.
The Jewish community, Jewish Federation of Williamsport was, was responsible for me to getting, getting me settled here.
So they found me a job.
Actually, I worked in the supermarket, because I wanted to really work on the paper.
But then I-- and I went to the paper.
And they interviewed me.
And I have the interview here, you know, that they-- I asked them for a job.
But they didn't give me a job.
So then the next thing, I worked for a furniture company where I published with a man.
The owner of the company was very ambitious.
And he wanted to have somebody, like a journalist, put out like a house organ, a company newspaper.
And it was an ideal fit for me.
And you knew English well enough to do that?
Oh yeah.
Well, I knew it, yet I, I worked with a woman.
And it was actually one of the office managers who became my co-editor.
She edited everything, so we-- it was all right, you know?
And I went to college, you know?
I went to Lycoming College, like I told you, and took postgraduate courses there.
Because like I said, previously, I had gone to the University of Munich and studied journalism when I was still in Germany, up until '51.
So it worked out pretty good.
I, I was there for three years.
But it was a small city, a small town.
I was, I was raised in Berlin, which was-- and again, it's one of the big metropolises in the, in, in, in the world.
So it was kind of boring.
And I didn't think I could make, really, my-- find my professional future there.
So I got friendly with the rabbi of the, of the, of the conservative congregation.
And he-- his wife was from Hungary.
And she spoke German.
That was probably one of the reasons why I liked them.
And they invited me over to dinner practically every Sunday.
So and I said to him, you know, rabbi, I, I really want to get back into journalism.
I already worked as a journalist in Germany.
What I need is to get a, get my foot in the door here somewhere.
He says, well, he says, my cousin is the editor of the Atlantic City, New Jersey press.
He says, when you're ready, and when I think you're ready, I'm just going to-- that was before computers and, and a lot of telephones-- I'll send him, I'll drop him a note.
And I ask him to see-- that you can see him.
I did, in 1940-- in 1954 I was tired of Williamsport.
I took my vacation from the furniture company's PR job to travel to-- walk in the office-- in the editor's office in Atlantic City, not knowing anybody, not knowing him.
He talks to me.
And he says, when can you start-- just like that.
I had anticipated that he would tell me, well, I don't really have a job right now for you.
But go to the I know somebody there or there.
He says, when can you start?
What was the name of his cousin, the rabbi, who got you the job?
Yeah, the rabbi?
Harry Schwartz.
And this man's name was Stanley Fink.
So Stan says, says to me, when can you start?
I said, well, I've got to give these people in Williamsport-- they've treated me very nicely-- at least two weeks.
So he even found me a place in the summer time, which is very hard in-- to get in Atlantic City back in the '50s, when it was in the world's playground, before casinos and everything.
It was a nice place.
He came.
And he found me a job.
And I start-- I said, don't you, don't you, don't you worry about me when I'm sitting on the rewrite desk and people from the public call in, and I'm answering, and I have an accent?
He says, forget.
I, I always thought people that were educated in Europe have a better education than, than we have in the US, which is not, not true any more, you know?
But it was true in those days, you know?
So I had no qualms to and, and worries that I couldn't do it.
And he-- in fact, the first day I reported to work, he walks me over from his office to the main news desk where all the editors are sitting.
And he says to me, as we walk there, he says, what name do you go by, he says.
And I said, gee, I, I go-- my name is Ludwig or Ludwig Joseph.
He says, well, he says, for some reason, it sounds a little-- not strange.
I don't know what word he used.
But he says it doesn't just sound catchy enough.
He says, what is the English translation of, of Ludwig?
And I said, I believe it's Louis-- L-O-U-I-S.
He says, why don't you call yourself Lou?
He gave me my name.
[INAUDIBLE] And is that where--
Yeah, so that nobody-- yeah, and most of the people in [INAUDIBLE], including my family, everybody knows me as Lou.
And I, I accept that very much.
Because I wanted to move away as much from my German, you know, connections, my German leanings, as much as possible.
Not, you know, not that I'm not-- shouldn't be-- not proud of your name.
But it, it, it goes better with the, with the flow of, you know, of words in this country.
Yeah, she was in-- she was a-- I was single then.
And I, I got friendly with the reporter, who was also Jewish-- Tony Schwartz was his name, no relation to the rabbi.
And he, he had a, 1955, he had a brand new Chevy Impala convertible, sharpest looking car, two-tone car.
I had a car then, too.
I had bought myself an old Studebaker Champion '50.
But he-- yeah, he and I, we got out double dating.
And one day, I'm-- I was on a date with my wife, who was an ad tech at the newspaper in the, in the advertising office.
And that's how it started.
Yeah.
Right.
No, you know, yeah, the we-- no, I still continued working for the paper until 1961.
And then I wanted to-- then I, I wanted to move to a bigger paper.
But it was very difficult.
I, I tried out I had a number-- Providence, Rhode Island, [INAUDIBLE].
Nothing seemed like a major improvement as far as economics and-- although status was concerned.
So I said, I'm going to have to look for a PR job.
And that's how I got my-- Here.
The job with the American Dental Association.
They wanted somebody to, to work with a, to work with the new media, that was a news man him or herself.
And that was Chicago.
And I [? moved ?] [? here. ?] They invited me to come out.
And they made me a very good offer.
And I also thought, gee, I'm back in a big city, you know?
And that-- this is exactly what I'm looking for.
Atlantic City is nice.
This is a resort, and now with the gambling.
But that was not [INAUDIBLE].
And that-- so then I became-- then I worked for them for 20 years.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah, I've been-- well, I've been several times.

In 1971, the city of Berlin-- I don't know if they still have it, but they had it in the '60s and '70s-- they had a program that was financed by the city of Berlin that brought back, for one week, former Berliners, regardless wherever they are-- in Australia, US, and you know-- on a one week's visit and getting the royal carpet treatment.
And the spouses are invited.
So that was one-- the only time that I really took my wife back.
We were both invited in 1971.
And we were given the red carpet lunches with the mayor, and so forth.
Yes, it was mostly focused on Jews.
But it could have also been people that were political enemies.

Because later on, when I worked for that American paper, which was run by all these former German and by these ex-Germans that were now in American uniform, they were-- some of the people were not Jewish.
They were Social Democrats.
They were just as much on the enemy list as Jews.
I mean, and they were sent to concentration camps.
So they're not necessarily were all, all Jews.
But mainly, they were Jews, you know?
Yeah.
So--
No, no, I went back there, I went back there in '71.
That was when still-- when this was still the Russian zone.
They gave us-- every day, they entertained us.
They put us up in, in first class hotels, gave us pocket-- you know, expense money every day to spend, you know?
And we were just treated as royalty-- wonderful program.
We were the youngest couple on that, on that flight.
It was a charter flight from New York directly to Berlin.
And these people were all old.
I mean, they were all-- I was then, what, 40 years old.
You know, my wife was in her 30s.
And we were youngest.
There were people in the 60s, 70s, and 80s, somewhere and, you know, only walking around with a cane.
Because they, they had been here for a long time in the US, or in-- and they were just-- you know, it was just the-- I, I don't think-- they went by age, really.
The only reason I got on this early in 1971 was because the, the press attache in the New York office, in the New York German embassy, was a former colleague of mine at that newspaper.
He worked on that.
And we were, we were friends.
And he-- when I found out that he was stationed in New York and we got together, he says, why don't you-- don't you want to go back to Berlin?
It's a free trip, you know, and everything.
You get the royal treatment.
So he put me on the-- he had the, he had the authority to put it on the list.
So and so then I worked, you know, with the ADA for 20 years.
In 19-- For the ADA, the American Dental Association?
Yeah, the American Dental Association-- then in 1981, I left them for Hill and Knowlton, one of the major public relations-- US public relations firms.
Very, very little.
I, we belong to the-- you know, I get the Jewish newspaper.
And I-- Jewish Appeal and so forth.
What is it?
The--
JAH.
Oh, yeah, the Appeal.
The federation or whatever, yeah.
No, I haven't joined.
I go to services, you know, but that-- you know, only on high holy days.
And of course, you know I am-- it's, it's, it's somewhat difficult because everybody in my family is Catholic, you know?
But all of them, they're not going to church.
But my wife works for the Catholic church, so she is, you know, she is, she is very much involved in it.
Oh yeah, yeah, they know.
But yeah-- but she doesn't-- you know, we don't talk much about it.
But-- so I have, you know, I have a lot of good experiences.
And--
Yeah, with, with Jewish, yeah, with Jewish people.
Yeah, I'd like to make-- can I--
Yeah.
Yeah.
OK.
Yeah.
Thank you.
Yeah.
